2 min read
Just now

--

Speed isn’t a setting you switch on at the end. It’s a decision you make at the start.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Speed isn’t a setting you switch on at the end of a build. It’s a decision you make at the start — and it’s the decision most portfolio templates get wrong.
Open the dev tools on a typical “premium” portfolio template, and you’ll often find the same story: several megabytes of transfer, a stack of animation libraries, fonts loading from a third party, and a layout that jolts as everything pops into place. It demos beautifully on a fast laptop and falls apart on a phone — which is exactly where the people judging your work will see it.
The page-builder tax
Most of that weight isn’t the designer’s fault; it’s the tool’s. Page builders earn their flexibility by generating layers of extra markup and script, and that flexibility has a cost the visitor pays in load time. For a lot of sites, that trade-off is fine. For a portfolio, whose entire job is to signal craft, shipping a bloated page is a contradiction the visitor feels even if they can’t name it.
Fast is an architecture, not a plugin
The alternative isn’t a faster plugin; it’s a different starting point. Hand-coded, semantic HTML with minimal JavaScript, images optimized and sized so nothing shifts, fonts self-hosted, and server rendering where it counts. None of that is exotic — it’s just decided early, when it’s cheap, instead of bolted on late, when it’s nearly impossible. That’s the whole difference between a template that passes Core Web Vitals and one that fights them forever.
Why it compounds
A fast foundation pays off twice. Google treats Core Web Vitals as a ranking signal, and the same server-rendered HTML that loads fast is what AI engines read most reliably when they decide what to cite. Start fast, and every page you build inherits the advantage. Start slow, and you spend the next year trying to claw it back.
If you want that head start, run any DesignToCodes portfolio template through PageSpeed and see for yourself.
